title: Database connection pool exhaustion
diagnostic_steps: On the database host run 'SHOW PROCESSLIST' and 'SHOW STATUS LIKE "Threads_connected"' to confirm connections are pinned at the configured ceiling.
Compare Threads_connected against max_connections; a flat line exactly at the limit indicates pool exhaustion, not organic load.
Correlate the onset timestamp with the most recent config change (for example CHG-* that lowered max_connections or pool size).
Check application-tier logs for 'Too many connections' or 'Aborted connection' errors pointing at this database.
remediation: If a recent config change reduced the limit, roll the change back to restore the previous max_connections / pool size.
Raise max_connections and the application connection-pool ceiling to a validated value, then reload without a full restart if supported.
Kill leaked or long-idle sessions and enable a connection-idle timeout to prevent recurrence.
Add pool-saturation alerting at 80 percent of the ceiling so the next event is caught before saturation.
verification: Threads_connected drops below 80 percent of max_connections and downstream latency returns to its pre-incident baseline.
No new 'Too many connections' errors appear in application logs for one full traffic cycle.

title: Database capacity exhaustion (CPU, memory, IO)
diagnostic_steps: Inspect host CPU, memory and disk IO utilisation (top, vmstat, iostat) at the anomaly onset window.
Run 'SHOW FULL PROCESSLIST' and the slow-query log to find long-running or unindexed queries.
Check buffer-pool hit ratio and swap usage to distinguish memory pressure from CPU saturation.
remediation: Terminate or throttle the offending queries and add the missing indexes identified in diagnostics.
Fail over to a replica or scale the instance vertically if saturation is sustained.
Introduce read-replica offloading or a query cache for the hot read path.
verification: CPU and IO utilisation fall back within baseline and slow-query count returns to normal.
Downstream application latency recovers to its pre-incident median.

title: DDoS / volumetric flood at the load balancer
diagnostic_steps: Check the load balancer for a sharp spike in new connections per second and SYN backlog (netstat -s, ss -s).
Identify whether traffic concentrates on a small set of source IPs or subnets versus broadly distributed sources.
Confirm upstream links are saturated while backend application CPU stays comparatively low (a signature of edge flooding).
remediation: Enable rate limiting and SYN-cookie protection on the load balancer; drop or tarpit the abusive source ranges.
Engage upstream scrubbing or the DDoS mitigation provider for volumetric absorption.
Temporarily raise connection and file-descriptor limits to ride out the burst without dropping legitimate traffic.
verification: New-connection rate returns to baseline and legitimate request success rate recovers.
SYN backlog drains and no socket-exhaustion errors remain in the load balancer log.

title: Link degradation / fiber path disruption
diagnostic_steps: Run 'show interfaces' on the affected switch and read error, CRC and discard counters on the transport interface.
Measure packet loss and jitter across the path (ping, mtr) and compare against the healthy baseline.
Check optical light levels / SFP diagnostics (show interface transceiver) for a degraded or failing optic.
remediation: Fail traffic over to the redundant path or LAG member and drain the degraded link.
Replace the failing optic or patch cable; open a carrier ticket if the degradation is on the provider span.
Clear interface counters after repair and monitor for recurring CRC growth.
verification: Packet loss and jitter return to baseline and CRC/error counters stop incrementing.
Throughput on the path recovers to expected capacity.

title: NIC failure / interface dropping frames
diagnostic_steps: Read interface error and discard counters on the host and the connected switch port (show interfaces counters errors).
Check dmesg / kernel ring buffer for NIC resets, link flaps or driver errors.
Test the port with a known-good host or move the host to a spare port to isolate NIC versus port versus cable.
remediation: Move the workload to the standby NIC or bond member; disable the faulty interface.
Replace the NIC or reseat/replace the cable and transceiver; update the NIC driver/firmware if implicated.
Rebalance the bond and re-enable the interface once counters are clean.
verification: Error and discard counters stay flat after the swap and packet loss clears.
The interface holds link with no flaps for a sustained monitoring window.

title: Port scan / reconnaissance sweep
diagnostic_steps: Look for a burst of short-lived connections to many distinct ports from one or few source IPs in flow/connection logs.
Correlate with IDS/IPS alerts and firewall deny logs for the same source over the onset window.
Confirm the pattern is scanning (many ports, little payload) rather than legitimate high-volume traffic.
remediation: Block or rate-limit the scanning source at the firewall / edge ACL and add it to the blocklist.
Verify no scanned service unexpectedly accepted a connection; close or firewall any exposed ports that should not be public.
Raise a security ticket and preserve the flow logs for follow-up.
verification: Scan traffic from the source is dropped at the edge and connection-count noise returns to baseline.
No unexpected service exposure remains after the ACL review.

title: Bad configuration push
diagnostic_steps: Identify the config change (CHG-*) whose timestamp immediately precedes the symptom onset and read its before/after diff.
Confirm the changed parameter plausibly explains the observed degradation (for example a lowered limit, wrong endpoint, or disabled feature).
Check whether the change was deployed to all instances or only the affected subset.
remediation: Roll the change back to the last-known-good configuration and redeploy.
If rollback is not possible, apply a corrected value and push through the standard change pipeline.
Add a pre-deploy validation check for the parameter class that caused the regression.
verification: The symptom clears within one propagation cycle of the rollback and metrics return to baseline.
A post-change health check passes on every instance that received the change.

title: DNS resolution failure or misconfiguration
diagnostic_steps: Query the DNS resolver directly (dig / nslookup) for the failing record and compare against the authoritative zone.
Check for a recent zone or resolver config change (TTL, record edit, forwarder change) around the onset.
Confirm whether failures are NXDOMAIN, SERVFAIL or timeouts to localise the fault (record vs resolver vs upstream).
remediation: Correct or roll back the erroneous DNS record / resolver configuration and flush caches.
Point clients at a healthy secondary resolver while the primary is repaired.
Lower the TTL temporarily so corrections propagate quickly.
verification: Direct resolver queries return the correct record and application error rate driven by lookups recovers.
No SERVFAIL/NXDOMAIN spikes remain across the resolver fleet.

title: General elevated latency triage
diagnostic_steps: Walk the dependency path from the symptomatic component upstream and compare per-hop latency to isolate where the increase originates.
Separate network latency (ping/traceroute) from service latency (application timing) at each hop.
Check for saturation (CPU, connections, IO) or a recent change on the first upstream hop that shows elevated latency.
remediation: Address the root hop specifically (scale, roll back, or clear the saturating resource) rather than the symptomatic victim.
Shed or queue non-critical load to protect the hot path while the root cause is fixed.
verification: P99 latency at the symptom returns to baseline once the identified upstream hop recovers.

title: General packet loss triage
diagnostic_steps: Measure loss per hop (mtr) to localise the lossy link or device.
Inspect interface discard/error counters and queue drops on the suspect device.
Check for congestion (throughput near capacity) versus hardware faults (CRC/errors).
remediation: Reroute around the lossy link or expand capacity if the cause is congestion.
Replace failing hardware/optics if the loss is driven by errors, then clear counters.
verification: Per-hop loss returns to zero and TCP retransmit rate normalises.

title: Connection / socket saturation
diagnostic_steps: Check open connections and TIME_WAIT/ephemeral-port usage (ss -s, netstat) against configured limits.
Identify whether saturation is inbound (clients) or outbound (to a dependency) to place the fault correctly.
Correlate with any limit change or a downstream slowdown causing connections to pile up.
remediation: Raise file-descriptor and connection limits to a validated ceiling and enable connection reuse / keep-alive.
Fix the downstream slowdown that is causing back-pressure if saturation is a symptom rather than the cause.
Tune TIME_WAIT reuse and ephemeral-port range if port exhaustion is confirmed.
verification: Active connection count falls below the ceiling and no new socket-exhaustion errors are logged.

title: CPU saturation on a service host
diagnostic_steps: Identify the top CPU consumers (top, pidstat) and whether load is user, system or IO-wait dominated.
Correlate with a traffic surge, a recent deploy, or a runaway process/thread.
Check run-queue length and context-switch rate for scheduler contention.
remediation: Roll back a regressing deploy, kill a runaway process, or scale out to add capacity.
Apply request rate limiting to protect the service while capacity is added.
verification: CPU utilisation returns within baseline and request latency recovers.

title: Memory pressure / leak
diagnostic_steps: Track resident memory growth over time (RSS trend) to distinguish a leak from a one-off spike.
Check for OOM-killer events in the kernel log and swap-in/out activity.
Correlate memory growth with a recent deploy or a specific request pattern.
remediation: Restart the leaking process to reclaim memory as an immediate mitigation, then roll back the introducing change.
Set memory limits and add heap/allocation profiling to find the leak source.
verification: Resident memory stabilises and swap activity ceases; no further OOM events occur.

title: Application error-rate spike (5xx)
diagnostic_steps: Break down errors by status code and endpoint to localise the failing dependency or code path.
Read application and dependency logs around the onset for the dominant exception signature.
Correlate with a deploy, a config change, or a downstream dependency degradation.
remediation: Roll back the implicated deploy/config or fail over the failing dependency.
Enable a circuit breaker / graceful degradation for the failing dependency to contain blast radius.
verification: Error rate returns to baseline and the dominant exception disappears from logs.

title: Application thread-pool / worker exhaustion
diagnostic_steps: Check active vs max worker threads and request-queue depth in application metrics.
Determine whether workers are blocked on a slow downstream call (database, cache, external API).
Look for a rising queue with flat throughput, the signature of pool exhaustion behind a slow dependency.
remediation: Fix or fail over the slow downstream dependency causing the block, which is usually the real root cause.
Increase the worker pool and queue bounds to a validated size and add timeouts to blocked calls.
verification: Queue depth drains, worker utilisation falls, and latency recovers to baseline.

title: Cache eviction storm / cold cache
diagnostic_steps: Check cache hit ratio and eviction rate; a sudden hit-ratio drop points to a cold or thrashing cache.
Correlate with a cache restart, a TTL/config change, or a key-space surge.
Confirm the downstream database load rose in step with the cache miss increase.
remediation: Warm the cache (pre-load hot keys) and restore the previous eviction/TTL policy if a change caused thrashing.
Scale cache memory or shard the key space to reduce evictions.
verification: Hit ratio recovers, database read load falls back, and latency normalises.

title: TLS / certificate failure
diagnostic_steps: Check certificate validity and expiry on the endpoint (openssl s_client) and the chain of trust.
Look for handshake failures in the proxy/load-balancer logs and a spike in TLS errors at the onset.
Correlate with a recent cert rotation or config change.
remediation: Replace the expired or misissued certificate and reload the terminating proxy.
Roll back a broken cert/config rotation and re-run the rotation through the validated pipeline.
verification: Handshakes succeed, the TLS error rate clears, and the served certificate is valid with a healthy expiry margin.

title: Disk IO saturation
diagnostic_steps: Measure device utilisation and await time (iostat -x) to confirm the disk is the bottleneck.
Identify the heaviest IO process (iotop, pidstat -d) and whether it is reads, writes or fsync-bound.
Check for a full or near-full filesystem and log/backup jobs running at the onset.
remediation: Throttle or reschedule the heavy IO job; move hot data to faster storage.
Free disk space and rotate logs; add IO capacity or provisioned IOPS if sustained.
verification: Device await and utilisation return to baseline and IO-bound latency recovers.

title: Routing / BGP flap
diagnostic_steps: Check routing-adjacency and BGP session state (show ip bgp summary) for flapping neighbours around the onset.
Read logs for repeated session up/down events and route withdrawals.
Correlate flaps with an underlying link error or a recent routing-policy change.
remediation: Stabilise or dampen the flapping session; fix the underlying link error driving the flaps.
Roll back an errant routing-policy change and re-advertise the correct routes.
verification: BGP sessions hold steady, routes are stable, and reachability/loss returns to baseline.

title: Load-balancer health-check failures / backend eviction
diagnostic_steps: Check which backends the load balancer has marked unhealthy and the health-check failure reason.
Verify whether the backends are truly down or the health-check threshold/path was changed.
Assess remaining healthy capacity versus current load to see if the survivors are now overloaded.
remediation: Restore or roll back a bad health-check configuration; repair and re-add genuinely failed backends.
Add capacity or shed load if too few backends remain healthy to serve current traffic.
verification: Backend pool returns to full healthy count and error rate recovers with headroom on the survivors.

title: Elevated TCP retransmissions
diagnostic_steps: Read TCP retransmit and out-of-order counters (netstat -s, nstat) at the affected host.
Localise the lossy segment with per-hop loss measurement (mtr).
Distinguish congestion-driven loss from a physical-layer fault via interface error counters.
remediation: Resolve the underlying loss (reroute congestion or replace failing hardware).
Tune TCP parameters only as a stopgap; the durable fix is removing the loss source.
verification: Retransmit rate falls to baseline and effective throughput recovers.
